question:
Curves $A,B,C$ and $D$ are defined in the plane as follows:
\begin{align*}
A &= \left\{ (x,y): x^2-y^2 = \frac{x}{x^2+y^2} \right\}, \\
B &= \left\{ (x,y): 2xy + \frac{y}{x^2+y^2} = 3 \right\}, \\
C &= \left\{ (x,y): x^3-3xy^2+3y=1 \right\}, \\
D &= \left\{ (x,y): 3x^2 y - 3x - y^3 = 0\right\}.
\end{align*}
Prove that $A \cap B = C \cap D$.
solution:
Solution 1. Let \( z=x+i y \). The equations defining \( A \) and \( B \) are the real and imaginary parts of the equation \( z^{2}=z^{-1}+3 i \), and similarly the equations defining \( C \) and \( D \) are the real and imaginary parts of \( z^{3}-3 i z=1 \). Hence for all real \( x \) and \( y \), we have
\[
(x, y) \in A \cap B \Longleftrightarrow z^{2}=z^{-1}+3 i \Longleftrightarrow z^{3}-3 i z=1 \Longleftrightarrow(x, y) \in C \cap D
\]

Thus \( A \cap B=C \cap D \).
Solution 2. Let \( F=x^{2}-y^{2}-\frac{x}{x^{2}+y^{2}}, G=2 x y+\frac{y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}-3, H=x^{3}-3 x y^{2}+3 y-1 \), and \( J=3 x^{2} y-3 x-y^{3} \) be the rational functions whose sets of zeros are \( A, B, C \), and \( D \), respectively. The identity
\[
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
x & -y \\
y & x
\end{array}\right)\binom{F}{G}=\binom{H}{J}
\]
shows immediately that \( F=G=0 \) implies \( H=J=0 \). Conversely if \( H=J=0 \) at \( (x, y) \), then \( (x, y) \neq(0,0) \), so \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}x & -y \\ y & x\end{array}\right)=x^{2}+y^{2} \) is nonzero, so (1) implies \( F=G=0 \) at \( (x, y) \).

Remark. Solution 1 multiplies \( z^{2}-z^{-1}-3 i \) by \( z \) to obtain \( z^{3}-3 i z-1 \). Solution 2 is simply doing this key step in terms of real and imaginary parts, so it is really the same solution, less elegantly written.

descriptive_long.question:
Curves $curvea,curveb,curvec$ and $curved$ are defined in the plane as follows:
\begin{align*}
curvea &= \left\{ (horizcoord,vertcoord): horizcoord^2-vertcoord^2 = \frac{horizcoord}{horizcoord^2+vertcoord^2} \right\}, \\
curveb &= \left\{ (horizcoord,vertcoord): 2horizcoord vertcoord + \frac{vertcoord}{horizcoord^2+vertcoord^2} = 3 \right\}, \\
curvec &= \left\{ (horizcoord,vertcoord): horizcoord^3-3horizcoord vertcoord^2+3vertcoord=1 \right\}, \\
curved &= \left\{ (horizcoord,vertcoord): 3horizcoord^2 vertcoord - 3horizcoord - vertcoord^3 = 0\right\}.
\end{align*}
Prove that $curvea \cap curveb = curvec \cap curved$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( complexz=horizcoord+i vertcoord \). The equations defining \( curvea \) and \( curveb \) are the real and imaginary parts of the equation \( complexz^{2}=complexz^{-1}+3 i \), and similarly the equations defining \( curvec \) and \( curved \) are the real and imaginary parts of \( complexz^{3}-3 i complexz=1 \). Hence for all real \( horizcoord \) and \( vertcoord \), we have
\[
(horizcoord, vertcoord) \in curvea \cap curveb \Longleftrightarrow complexz^{2}=complexz^{-1}+3 i \Longleftrightarrow complexz^{3}-3 i complexz=1 \Longleftrightarrow(horizcoord, vertcoord) \in curvec \cap curved
\]

Thus \( curvea \cap curveb=curvec \cap curved \).

Solution 2. Let \( functionf=horizcoord^{2}-vertcoord^{2}-\frac{horizcoord}{horizcoord^{2}+vertcoord^{2}},\; functiong=2 horizcoord vertcoord+\frac{vertcoord}{horizcoord^{2}+vertcoord^{2}}-3,\; functionh=horizcoord^{3}-3 horizcoord vertcoord^{2}+3 vertcoord-1 \), and \( functionj=3 horizcoord^{2} vertcoord-3 horizcoord-vertcoord^{3} \) be the rational functions whose sets of zeros are \( curvea, curveb, curvec \), and \( curved \), respectively. The identity
\[
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
horizcoord & -vertcoord \\
vertcoord & horizcoord
\end{array}\right)\binom{functionf}{functiong}=\binom{functionh}{functionj}
\]
shows immediately that \( functionf=functiong=0 \) implies \( functionh=functionj=0 \). Conversely if \( functionh=functionj=0 \) at \( (horizcoord, vertcoord) \), then \( (horizcoord, vertcoord) \neq(0,0) \), so \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}horizcoord & -vertcoord \\ vertcoord & horizcoord\end{array}\right)=horizcoord^{2}+vertcoord^{2} \) is nonzero, so (1) implies \( functionf=functiong=0 \) at \( (horizcoord, vertcoord) \).

Remark. Solution 1 multiplies \( complexz^{2}-complexz^{-1}-3 i \) by \( complexz \) to obtain \( complexz^{3}-3 i complexz-1 \). Solution 2 is simply doing this key step in terms of real and imaginary parts, so it is really the same solution, less elegantly written.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Curves $lamplight,starwheel,umbrella$ and $ridgepass$ are defined in the plane as follows:
\begin{align*}
lamplight &= \left\{ (marigold,sapphire): marigold^{2}-sapphire^{2} = \frac{marigold}{marigold^{2}+sapphire^{2}} \right\}, \\
starwheel &= \left\{ (marigold,sapphire): 2 marigold sapphire + \frac{sapphire}{marigold^{2}+sapphire^{2}} = 3 \right\}, \\
umbrella &= \left\{ (marigold,sapphire): marigold^{3}-3 marigold sapphire^{2}+3 sapphire=1 \right\}, \\
ridgepass &= \left\{ (marigold,sapphire): 3 marigold^{2} sapphire - 3 marigold - sapphire^{3} = 0\right\}.
\end{align*}
Prove that $lamplight \cap starwheel = umbrella \cap ridgepass$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( peregrine=marigold+i sapphire \). The equations defining \( lamplight \) and \( starwheel \) are the real and imaginary parts of the equation \( peregrine^{2}=peregrine^{-1}+3 i \), and similarly the equations defining \( umbrella \) and \( ridgepass \) are the real and imaginary parts of \( peregrine^{3}-3 i peregrine=1 \). Hence for all real marigold and sapphire, we have
\[
(marigold, sapphire) \in lamplight \cap starwheel \Longleftrightarrow peregrine^{2}=peregrine^{-1}+3 i \Longleftrightarrow peregrine^{3}-3 i peregrine=1 \Longleftrightarrow(marigold, sapphire) \in umbrella \cap ridgepass
\]

Thus \( lamplight \cap starwheel=umbrella \cap ridgepass \).
Solution 2. Let \( topazstone=marigold^{2}-sapphire^{2}-\frac{marigold}{marigold^{2}+sapphire^{2}}, quillshade=2 marigold sapphire+\frac{sapphire}{marigold^{2}+sapphire^{2}}-3, berrybrook=marigold^{3}-3 marigold sapphire^{2}+3 sapphire-1 \), and \( duskpetal=3 marigold^{2} sapphire-3 marigold-sapphire^{3} \) be the rational functions whose sets of zeros are \( lamplight, starwheel, umbrella \), and \( ridgepass \), respectively. The identity
\[
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
marigold & -sapphire \\
sapphire & marigold
\end{array}\right)\binom{topazstone}{quillshade}=\binom{berrybrook}{duskpetal}
\]
shows immediately that \( topazstone=quillshade=0 \) implies \( berrybrook=duskpetal=0 \). Conversely if \( berrybrook=duskpetal=0 \) at \( (marigold, sapphire) \), then \( (marigold, sapphire) \neq(0,0) \), so \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}marigold & -sapphire \\ sapphire & marigold\end{array}\right)=marigold^{2}+sapphire^{2} \) is nonzero, so (1) implies \( topazstone=quillshade=0 \) at \( (marigold, sapphire) \).

Remark. Solution 1 multiplies \( peregrine^{2}-peregrine^{-1}-3 i \) by \( peregrine \) to obtain \( peregrine^{3}-3 i peregrine-1 \). Solution 2 is simply doing this key step in terms of real and imaginary parts, so it is really the same solution, less elegantly written.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Curves $noncurveone,noncurvetwo,noncurvethree$ and $noncurvefour$ are defined in the plane as follows:
\begin{align*}
noncurveone &= \left\{ (fixedvertical,knownhorizontal): fixedvertical^2-knownhorizontal^2 = \frac{fixedvertical}{fixedvertical^2+knownhorizontal^2} \right\}, \\
noncurvetwo &= \left\{ (fixedvertical,knownhorizontal): 2 fixedvertical knownhorizontal + \frac{knownhorizontal}{fixedvertical^2+knownhorizontal^2} = 3 \right\}, \\
noncurvethree &= \left\{ (fixedvertical,knownhorizontal): fixedvertical^3-3 fixedvertical knownhorizontal^2+3 knownhorizontal=1 \right\}, \\
noncurvefour &= \left\{ (fixedvertical,knownhorizontal): 3 fixedvertical^2 knownhorizontal - 3 fixedvertical - knownhorizontal^3 = 0\right\}.
\end{align*}
Prove that $noncurveone \cap noncurvetwo = noncurvethree \cap noncurvefour$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( realcoordinate=fixedvertical+i knownhorizontal \). The equations defining \( noncurveone \) and \( noncurvetwo \) are the real and imaginary parts of the equation \( realcoordinate^{2}=realcoordinate^{-1}+3 i \), and similarly the equations defining \( noncurvethree \) and \( noncurvefour \) are the real and imaginary parts of \( realcoordinate^{3}-3 i realcoordinate=1 \). Hence for all real \( fixedvertical \) and \( knownhorizontal \), we have
\[
(fixedvertical, knownhorizontal) \in noncurveone \cap noncurvetwo \Longleftrightarrow realcoordinate^{2}=realcoordinate^{-1}+3 i \Longleftrightarrow realcoordinate^{3}-3 i realcoordinate=1 \Longleftrightarrow(fixedvertical, knownhorizontal) \in noncurvethree \cap noncurvefour
\]

Thus \( noncurveone \cap noncurvetwo = noncurvethree \cap noncurvefour \).

Solution 2. Let \( nonfunctionone=fixedvertical^{2}-knownhorizontal^{2}-\frac{fixedvertical}{fixedvertical^{2}+knownhorizontal^{2}}, nonfunctiontwo=2 fixedvertical knownhorizontal+\frac{knownhorizontal}{fixedvertical^{2}+knownhorizontal^{2}}-3, nonfunctionthree=fixedvertical^{3}-3 fixedvertical knownhorizontal^{2}+3 knownhorizontal-1 \), and \( nonfunctionfour=3 fixedvertical^{2} knownhorizontal-3 fixedvertical-knownhorizontal^{3} \) be the rational functions whose sets of zeros are \( noncurveone, noncurvetwo, noncurvethree \), and \( noncurvefour \), respectively. The identity
\[
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
fixedvertical & -knownhorizontal \\
knownhorizontal & fixedvertical
\end{array}\right)\binom{nonfunctionone}{nonfunctiontwo}=\binom{nonfunctionthree}{nonfunctionfour}
\]
shows immediately that \( nonfunctionone=nonfunctiontwo=0 \) implies \( nonfunctionthree=nonfunctionfour=0 \). Conversely if \( nonfunctionthree=nonfunctionfour=0 \) at \( (fixedvertical, knownhorizontal) \), then \( (fixedvertical, knownhorizontal) \neq(0,0) \), so \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}fixedvertical & -knownhorizontal \\ knownhorizontal & fixedvertical\end{array}\right)=fixedvertical^{2}+knownhorizontal^{2} \) is nonzero, so (1) implies \( nonfunctionone=nonfunctiontwo=0 \) at \( (fixedvertical, knownhorizontal) \).

Remark. Solution 1 multiplies \( realcoordinate^{2}-realcoordinate^{-1}-3 i \) by \( realcoordinate \) to obtain \( realcoordinate^{3}-3 i realcoordinate-1 \). Solution 2 is simply doing this key step in terms of real and imaginary parts, so it is really the same solution, less elegantly written.

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
Curves $poilkjhg,asdfghjk,zxcvbnml$ and $qwertyui$ are defined in the plane as follows:
\begin{align*}
poilkjhg &= \left\{ (qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla): qzxwvtnp^2-hjgrksla^2 = \frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^2+hjgrksla^2} \right\}, \\
asdfghjk &= \left\{ (qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla): 2qzxwvtnp hjgrksla + \frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp^2+hjgrksla^2} = 3 \right\}, \\
zxcvbnml &= \left\{ (qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla): qzxwvtnp^3-3qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^2+3hjgrksla=1 \right\}, \\
qwertyui &= \left\{ (qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla): 3qzxwvtnp^2 hjgrksla - 3qzxwvtnp - hjgrksla^3 = 0\right\}.
\end{align*}
Prove that $poilkjhg \cap asdfghjk = zxcvbnml \cap qwertyui$.
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. Let \( mnbvcxqw=qzxwvtnp+i hjgrksla \). The equations defining \( poilkjhg \) and \( asdfghjk \) are the real and imaginary parts of the equation \( mnbvcxqw^{2}=mnbvcxqw^{-1}+3 i \), and similarly the equations defining \( zxcvbnml \) and \( qwertyui \) are the real and imaginary parts of \( mnbvcxqw^{3}-3 i mnbvcxqw=1 \). Hence for all real \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \), we have
\[
(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \in poilkjhg \cap asdfghjk \Longleftrightarrow mnbvcxqw^{2}=mnbvcxqw^{-1}+3 i \Longleftrightarrow mnbvcxqw^{3}-3 i mnbvcxqw=1 \Longleftrightarrow(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \in zxcvbnml \cap qwertyui
\]

Thus \( poilkjhg \cap asdfghjk=zxcvbnml \cap qwertyui \).
Solution 2. Let \( lkjhgfdp=qzxwvtnp^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}-\frac{qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}}, poiuytre=2 qzxwvtnp hjgrksla+\frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}}-3, mnbhgfdx=qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{2}+3 hjgrksla-1 \), and \( qazwsxed=3 qzxwvtnp^{2} hjgrksla-3 qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla^{3} \) be the rational functions whose sets of zeros are \( poilkjhg, asdfghjk, zxcvbnml \), and \( qwertyui \), respectively. The identity
\[
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
qzxwvtnp & -hjgrksla \\
hjgrksla & qzxwvtnp
\end{array}\right)\binom{lkjhgfdp}{poiuytre}=\binom{mnbhgfdx}{qazwsxed}
\]
shows immediately that \( lkjhgfdp=poiuytre=0 \) implies \( mnbhgfdx=qazwsxed=0 \). Conversely if \( mnbhgfdx=qazwsxed=0 \) at \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \), then \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \neq(0,0) \), so \( \operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cc}qzxwvtnp & -hjgrksla \\ hjgrksla & qzxwvtnp\end{array}\right)=qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2} \) is nonzero, so (1) implies \( lkjhgfdp=poiuytre=0 \) at \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \).

Remark. Solution 1 multiplies \( mnbvcxqw^{2}-mnbvcxqw^{-1}-3 i \) by \( mnbvcxqw \) to obtain \( mnbvcxqw^{3}-3 i mnbvcxqw-1 \). Solution 2 is simply doing this key step in terms of real and imaginary parts, so it is really the same solution, less elegantly written.

kernel_variant.question:
Let

z = x + i y, \qquad (x,y) \in \mathbb R^{2}.

Define four real curves by

A = \bigl\{(x,y):\;x^{4}-6x^{2}y^{2}+y^{4}=\dfrac{2x}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\bigr\},

B = \bigl\{(x,y):\;4xy\,(x^{2}-y^{2})+\dfrac{2y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}=5\bigr\},

C = \bigl\{(x,y):\;x^{5}-10x^{3}y^{2}+5xy^{4}+5y=2\bigr\},

D = \bigl\{(x,y):\;5x^{4}y-10x^{2}y^{3}+y^{5}-5x=0\bigr\}.

(Proviso: the point (x,y) = (0,0) is excluded from the definitions of A and B so that the denominators are well defined.)

Prove that

                    A \cap B\;=\;C \cap D.
kernel_variant.solution:
Write z = x + i y with (x,y) \neq (0,0).

Step 1 -  Curves A and B arise from a single complex equation.
-------------------------------------------------------------
Compute the fourth power of z and the number 2\,/z + 5 i.

  z^{4} = (x + i y)^{4} = (x^{4} - 6 x^{2} y^{2} + y^{4})
                         \, + \, i\,[4 x y (x^{2} - y^{2})],

  2/z + 5 i = \dfrac{2(x - i y)}{x^{2}+y^{2}} + 5 i
            = \dfrac{2x}{x^{2}+y^{2}}
              \, + \, i\Bigl(5 - \dfrac{2y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}\Bigr).

Therefore z^{4} = 2/z + 5 i if and only if the real and imaginary
parts are equal, i.e.

   x^{4} - 6 x^{2} y^{2} + y^{4}        = \dfrac{2x}{x^{2}+y^{2}}  \;(\!*\!)
   4 x y (x^{2} - y^{2}) + \dfrac{2y}{x^{2}+y^{2}} = 5          \;(\!*\!*).

Conditions (\!*\!) and (\!*\!*) are exactly the equations that
define the curves A and B, respectively.  Hence

           (x,y) \in A \cap B    \Longleftrightarrow   z^{4} = 2/z + 5 i.   (1)

Step 2 -  Eliminate the fraction.
---------------------------------
Because z \neq 0, multiplying (1) by z gives

           z^{5} - 5 i z = 2.                                         (2)

Step 3 -  Expand (2) into real and imaginary parts.
---------------------------------------------------
First expand z^{5}:

  z^{5} = (x + i y)^{5}
        = (x^{5} - 10 x^{3} y^{2} + 5 x y^{4})
          \, + \, i\,[5 x^{4} y - 10 x^{2} y^{3} + y^{5}].

Next,  - 5 i z = 5 y \; - \; 5 i x.

Hence

  z^{5} - 5 i z
  = \bigl[x^{5} - 10 x^{3} y^{2} + 5 x y^{4} + 5 y\bigr]
    \, + \, i\bigl[5 x^{4} y - 10 x^{2} y^{3} + y^{5} - 5 x\bigr].

Equation (2) says that this complex number equals 2 (whose real part is 2 and whose imaginary part is 0).  Therefore

   x^{5} - 10 x^{3} y^{2} + 5 x y^{4} + 5 y = 2,                 (3)
   5 x^{4} y - 10 x^{2} y^{3} + y^{5} - 5 x = 0.                 (4)

Equation (3) is the defining relation for curve C, and (4) is the
defining relation for the (corrected) curve D.  Consequently

           (x,y) \in C \cap D    \Longleftrightarrow   z^{5} - 5 i z = 2.   (5)

Step 4 -  Equate the descriptions.
----------------------------------
From (1), (2) and (5) we have the chain of equivalences

   (x,y) \in A \cap B\; \Longleftrightarrow \;z^{4} = 2/z + 5 i
                      \Longleftrightarrow \;z^{5} - 5 i z = 2
                      \Longleftrightarrow \;(x,y) \in C \cap D.

Therefore A \cap B = C \cap D, as claimed. \hfill \Box

core_steps: Encode the point (x, y) as the complex number z = x + i y.
Observe that the equations for A and B are respectively the real and imaginary parts of a single complex relation E₁ :  z² = z⁻¹ + 3 i.
Because z ≠ 0, multiply E₁ by z to get the equivalent relation E₂ :  z³ − 3 i z = 1.
Note that the equations for C and D are exactly the real and imaginary parts of E₂, hence the solution sets of the two systems coincide: A ∩ B = C ∩ D.

slot1.description: The coefficient of the purely imaginary term (3 in 3 i) that appears in both complex equations and in curves B and D; any non-zero real constant would work.

slot2.description: The coefficient of z⁻¹ (1) which becomes the constant term on the right of the second complex equation and shows up in curves A and C; any non-zero real constant would work.

slot3.description: The exponent 2 in z² (and consequently the exponent 3 in z³ after multiplying by z); any integer n≥1, with the second exponent n+1, preserves the argument.
